story:
Sweat crawls down my cheek, but I keep my palms pressed to his face. If I lose contact, I lose the thread. He pleads, his voice warped and far away, like he’s shouting up a well. He offers coin. He calls me a dirty Synap. I walk the room—gilded walls, fat with noble comfort—and then I come back to him, hands on temples, and push deeper.

His mind flips to a crisp fall afternoon: mountains facing Krangston, birds spilling music, trees a blaze of crimson and brown. His wife. His child. It’s a soft place to hide secrets. I nudge the memory, not enough to break it, just enough to let it open. He reaches into his pocket and pulls a note.

Everything is going as planned. I have sent you a new batch of Magiberi. They will be traveling down the Trailwood path, all the way to Krangston. Once you’ve received them, I expect you to keep your end of the bargain.
K.R.

I let go, breathing hard. Magiberi—magic-born children—shipped like cargo. My stomach turns. “What is the North getting in return?” I ask.

Confusion flicks across his face. He thought I was the North’s hound. “I’m not here for King Salazar,” I say, letting the disgust show when I say the name. “I’m resistance.”

That word changes him. He laughs. He paints the picture generations of us already know: fields choked with bodies, peasants fed to wars, and now the kingdoms trading away gifted children for coin. He calls us plebes. He calls us fodder. He tells me I’ll die before I can use this.

I breathe, feel Paix lay calm over my nerves through our bond. I was taken as a child because of what I am—a Synaptic, trained until my nose bled, told to be grateful—and it forged a vow in me. No more children stolen. No more sold futures.

I hit him once, hard and precise. Bone cracks; his laughter breaks into blood. It’s not for satisfaction; it’s to make him stop and listen. The sound never leaves the room. Paix’s ward seals it tight.

“We’re going to keep this moment between us,” I say, and I mean it. I slip back into his mind and build a lock around what I found—around me, this room, this pain. If someone can break it, they deserve the truth. He blinks, dazed. “Who are you?” he asks, staring down at the ropes he can’t remember earning.

“Just a Synap,” I tell him, smiling. “Pleasant doing business, King.”

The window is open to a drop that would kill anyone else. I step into it. The fall is brief. Paix surges up beneath me, white scales flashing in the sun. White dragons are fast, and they feel everything. She worries; I’m drained from digging. I tell her I’m fine. She asks about my hand. Worth it, I say, and we climb.

Behind us, the King of the South stares out at a sky that hasn’t held a dragon in centuries, and now it holds mine. He’s seen the reason we’ll win. Not because we steal or bruise or threaten. Because we are no longer alone—Synaptic and dragon, rebel and friend—riding toward a world where children are not stock, and power is not owned.
